key_insight: Provable coverage of valuable mathematics requires a proof checker plus an unavoidable stream of certified trivia: without trivia, coverage is capped, but with even vanishing-rate trivia, it jumps to the optimal regime.
engineering_relevance: For theorem-proving systems, a perfect verifier ensures soundness but cannot replace taste, so generation pipelines may need to deliberately emit many correct-but-low-value statements to uncover unseen valuable results.
method: Model mathematical generation as nested language learning in the limit with a verifiable formal language and an unknown valuable sublanguage.
Analyze two regimes: generators with finitely many trivia versus generators allowing infinitely many trivia.
Prove sharp coverage bounds and tight dichotomy on the core/literature distribution, then instantiate the result in a compression model of mathematics.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical proofs and tight optimality results under an adversarial enumeration model, rather than empirical benchmark data.
watch_item: The conclusions depend on formal assumptions about the core density, adversarial presentation, and the definition of 'valuable' mathematics, so practical systems may not match the theorem's exact regime.
paper_title: Flood and Harvest: The Provable Necessity of Trivia for Generating Valuable Mathematics via the Lens of Language Generation in the Limit
